exemplar.title:
To what extent is the relationship between dopamine levels and the development of
non-substance use addictive behaviors in adults?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent is the relationship between dopamine levels and the development of
non-substance use addictive behaviors in adults?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, focused introduction and effective signposting throughout the essay
Well-defined topic communication with concise explanation of key concepts
Research methodology engages a suitable range of relevant sources
weaknesses: No explicit methodology section detailing search strategy or inclusion criteria
Research question wording is slightly awkward and scope remains broad
Some repetition of purpose statements reduces overall coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates the focus on dopamine and non-substance addictive behaviours accurately and employs a generally appropriate literature-review methodology. Headings and signposting keep the topic visible, and the research questionâwhile clearâis hampered by broad scope and awkward phrasing. The absence of a standalone methodology section and occasional redundancy prevent a perfect mark, but the overall work demonstrates effective focus and method, meriting 5/6.

strengths: Demonstrates good knowledge of neurobiological mechanisms and behavioural addiction phenomena
Terminology and specialized concepts (e.g. âventral striatumâ, âbinding potentialâ) are used accurately
Source application is mostly relevant with clear understanding of each studyâs contributions
weaknesses: Minor typographical errors in authorsâ names and statistical descriptions
Over-reliance on descriptive summaries limits deeper conceptual integration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows strong subject knowledge and generally sound use of terminology. Summaries of studies are accurate and mostly well-applied to the argument. However, frequent minor errors, an overemphasis on description, and lapses in statistical clarity keep the work out of the top band. A score of 4/6 reflects solid understanding with room for more precise, concise source integration.

strengths: Wide selection of relevant studies (PET, pharmacovigilance, animal models) well aligned to the research question
Reasoned evaluation contrasting supporting and counter-evidence
Numerous insightful suggestions for future research and methodological improvements
weaknesses: Analysis occasionally remains catalog-style with limited theoretical synthesis
Evaluation of ethical considerations and alternative explanations is superficial
Conclusion is brief and lacks detailed proposals for specific future designs
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student offers substantial critical engagement, selecting diverse, relevant research and evaluating strengths and limitations. Analysis goes beyond summary and the discussion is balanced, with recommendations to deepen statistical, ethical, and methodological rigour. Though the work falls short of consistently deep theoretical integration and a fully developed conclusion, the quality of critical thinking justifies a high mark of 10/12.

strengths: Recognizable essay structure with introduction, body, conclusion, and a works cited list
Basic formatting conventions respected including headings differentiation and provided word count
weaknesses: Missing key EE elements (abstract, methodology section) and table of contents lacks page numbers
Exceeds the 4 000-word limit and contains OCR artifacts in text
Inconsistent reference formatting and absent pagination hinder navigation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is generally appropriate but incomplete. The student follows basic structural conventions and supplies a word count, yet critical elementsâsuch as an abstract, clear pagination and a dedicated methodology sectionâare absent. Formatting errors and the word-limit overrun prevent higher marks, resulting in 2/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Psychology terms such as âdopamine agonist,â âventral striatum,â âbinding potential,â âMANOVAâ and âimpulse-control disordersâ are used correctly and consistently.  Key EE conventions (e.g. âcorrelationâ, âgeneralizabilityâ, âdemand characteristicsâ) appear appropriately.  Minor slips in grammar and occasional misuse of phrasing (e.g. âthe uses the PRRâ) stop the strand from being exemplary, but terminology is generally sound and therefore merits the 3-4 band.

Headings are clearly differentiated, basic typographical conventions are followed and a word count is provided.  Nevertheless, pagination is absent in the extract, several in-text formulae render incorrectly, and the work exceeds the 4 000-word limit (4 057 words).  Minor formatting issues in the references (inconsistent italics, broken URL) persist.  These mixed features place the essay at the top of the 1-2 band for layout and formatting.

The student demonstrates good knowledge of neurobiological mechanisms (dopamine release, D2/D3 receptor availability, transporters) and of behavioural addiction phenomena.  Summaries of individual studies are mostly accurate and relevant.  Application of sources is evident when strengths/limitations are linked to the overall argument.  Occasional inaccuracies (e.g. overstating generalisability, mis-typing authorsâ names) and an over-reliance on descriptive content prevent the work from reaching the highest band, but it comfortably meets the 3-4 descriptors.

The student uses a literature-review methodology, selecting PET studies, clinical case reports, epidemiological databases and animal research.  Source range is adequate and generally relevant; each studyâs design and limitations are identified, showing some informed selection.  Nevertheless, the method is not fully âcompleteâ: there is no explicit explanation of search strategy, inclusion criteria or data-synthesis procedure, and the inclusion of a generic AI website in the reference list signals uneven source vetting.  These factors keep the work in the 3-4 band.

The student clearly communicates the focus on âdopamine levels and non-substance use addictive behaviours among adults.â  The introduction explains why the issue is important, outlines key concepts (dopamine, reward circuitry, behavioural addictions) and specifies the adult population.  Headings and signposting keep the topic visible throughout.  Minor weaknessesâe.g. occasional repetition and a brief, formulaic conclusionâslightly lessen the overall effectiveness, so a solid but not perfect score in the top band is warranted.

The selected researchâhuman PET studies, pharmacovigilance databases, animal models and clinical observationsâaligns well with the question, showing breadth and mostly good relevance.  Each studyâs connection to dopamine and behavioural addiction is spelled out.  A few inclusions (e.g. general WHO statistic, AI website) are only tangentially relevant, preventing the highest band, but overall quality and relevance sit comfortably in the 4-6 range.

The research question is stated and generally focused: âTo what extent is the relationship between dopamine levels and the development of non-substance use addictive behaviours in adults?â  It establishes the variables and the target population, and the body of the essay does revolve around answering it.  However, the wording is grammatically awkward (âto what extent is the relationshipâ) and the scope remains broad, spanning many different behaviours (gaming, gambling, compulsive eating, shopping, hyper-sexuality).  This partial lack of precision places the work solidly in the 3-4 band rather than the highest band.

The essay follows a recognisable structure (introduction, supporting evidence, counter-evidence, conclusion, works cited) and includes a table of contents.  However, key EE elements such as an abstract and distinct methodology section are missing, and the table of contents lacks page numbers.  Some headings appear mid-sentence, suggesting uneven organisation.  These features correspond to a generally appropriate but incomplete structure, earning the upper mark of the 1-2 band.

A reasoned, if brief, overall position is offeredâthat dopamine is involved but not the sole factor.  The essay contrasts supporting and counter evidence and ends with suggestions for future research.  Yet the evaluation remains surface-level, with limited exploration of alternative explanations (e.g. environmental factors), little methodological triangulation, and a very short conclusion.  This partial critical evaluation fits the 4-6 band but does not achieve the depth required for 7-9.

Analysis goes beyond summary: the student evaluates strengths (control groups, imaging precision, sample sizes) and limitations (small N, gender bias, lack of controls).  However, the analysis is often catalogue-like, lacking integration or deeper theoretical interpretation.  There is limited synthesis across studies and little interrogation of contradictory findings.  Consequently, the work demonstrates clear but not consistently well-developed analysis, matching the lower half of the 4-6 band.

annotations.comment: The title at the reportâs start clearly states the focus, fulfilling the requirement to present the investigationâs topic immediately.
The stated word count of 4057 exceeds the 4000âword limit, risking penalties; it should be trimmed for compliance.

The research question wording âto what extent is the relationshipâ is awkward; revise for clarity and specificity, e.g. âHow does dopamine level variation influence non-substance addictive behaviours in adults?â
The table of contents lacks page numbers for each section, hindering navigation and reducing structural clarity.
An abstract summarizing scope, methodology, key findings, and conclusion is missing; include one to conform with IB EE conventions.
Headings in the table appear mid-sentence without uniform styling; ensure consistent heading levels and clear organization.
The introduction relies on a single WHO statistic without contextualizing recent trends or broader literature, limiting its evidential depth.
The OCR artifact â\overline{2}â appears at the end of the page; remove or replace with intended content.
An explicit methodology section outlining search strategy, inclusion criteria, and synthesis procedure is absent; add to enhance transparency.
The repeated restatement of the research question on page 4 is redundant; condense to maintain focus and flow.
Consolidate the purpose statements into a single, concise paragraph to avoid repetition and enhance coherence.
The position statement is clear and indicates the studentâs critical stance that dopamine is contributory but not sole, strengthening focus.
Nirenberg & Walters relies on weight gain as a proxy for dopamine without direct neurochemical measures; discuss this limitation.
The description of telephone interviews and chart reviews is vague; specify how weight measures were obtained and validated to strengthen clarity.
Discuss how observed weight gain mechanistically links to dopamine activity and neural pathways to deepen analysis.
The explanation of proportional reporting ratio (PRR) in Moore & Glenmullen is clear and well contextualized.
Include the formula $$PRR=\frac{a/(a+b)}{c/(c+d)}$$ when discussing proportional reporting ratio to provide precise mathematical clarity.
Critically assess reporting bias in the FDA database to deepen evaluation of how adverse events may be underreported.
The description of Ariatama et al. study ends abruptly, suggesting the text is incomplete; ensure full methods and results are presented.
No statistical significance values (p, r) are reported for the IGDâdepression correlation; include these to substantiate the findings.
The strengths note the use of IGDS9-SF and PHQ-9 assessment tools, demonstrating appropriate application of specialized measures.
The studyâs recruitment from internet cafes may introduce sampling bias; discuss its impact on generalizability.
To mitigate self-report bias, triangulate questionnaire data with observational or physiological measures in future research.
The OCR artifact â\overline{7}â appears again; remove it to maintain professional presentation.
Ethical considerations of exposing participants to PET radioisotopes are not discussed; address consent and safety protocols.
Clarify the ANOVA design, report degrees of freedom and p-values for the PET results to strengthen statistical transparency.
The ethical implications of animal stress in Salamone et al. are omitted; include discussion of welfare and consent alternatives.
The discussion effectively links dopamine function to motivation and high-effort tasks, reinforcing theoretical understanding.
Joutsa et al. correlations lack reported p-values and effect sizes; include them for rigorous evaluation of significance.
Noting high ecological validity in the gambling tasks demonstrates excellent understanding of study context.
The conclusion reiterates limitations but lacks concrete proposals for specific future study designs; detail longitudinal or experimental plans.
Propose a longitudinal mixed-methods study with diverse sampling and defined inclusion/exclusion criteria to address identified gaps.
The inclusion of ChatGPT and Flawlessly AI in works cited undermines academic credibility; replace with peer-reviewed sources.
References show inconsistent italics and URL formatting; apply a uniform citation style (e.g., APA or MLA) throughout.
Adopt a consistent citation format for all works cited entries to enhance professionalism and readability.
